---
title: "Wooldridge EACSPD -- Ch. 7: Estimating systems of equations by OLS and GLS (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Stata examples reproduced from [UCLA OARC — EACSPD Chapter 7](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/examples/eacspd/econometric-analysis-of-cross-section-and-panel-data-by-jeffrey-m-wooldridgechapter-7-estimating-systems-of-equations-by-ols-and-gls/).

Source text: Wooldridge, J. M., *Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel Data*, 2nd ed., MIT Press.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

The chapter uses three bundled EACSPD data sets, loaded **by name**:

```{julia}
fringe  = dataset("fringe")     # 616 x 39
jtrain1 = dataset("jtrain1")    # 471 x 30
gpa     = dataset("gpa")        # 732 x 26
size(fringe), size(jtrain1), size(gpa)
```

## Example 7.3 (fringe.dta) — SUR of earnings and benefits

Original Stata code:

```{julia}
#=
use http://www.stata.com/data/jwooldridge/eacsap/fringe
sureg (hrearn educ exper expersq tenure tenuresq union south nrtheast nrthcen married white male) ///
      (hrbens educ exper expersq tenure tenuresq union south nrtheast nrthcen married white male)
=#
```

Julia translation with `stata_sureg`:

```{julia}
X73 = [:educ, :exper, :expersq, :tenure, :tenuresq, :union,
       :south, :nrtheast, :nrthcen, :married, :white, :male]

sur73 = stata_sureg(fringe, :hrearn => X73, :hrbens => X73)
```

**Comparison with the published Stata output.** Exact agreement to all printed
digits, in both equations and in the summary block. Stata reports
`hrearn`: RMSE 4.3089, R² 0.2051; `hrbens`: RMSE 0.51526, R² 0.3987 — the same
values shown above. Spot checks: `educ` in the earnings equation
0.4588139 (SE 0.068393) vs Julia 0.4588139 (0.0683930); `union` in the benefits
equation 0.3659085 (SE 0.0482482) vs Julia 0.3659085 (0.0482482); `_cons` in the
earnings equation −2.632127 (SE 1.215291) vs Julia −2.6321267 (1.2152912).

A joint Wald test across both equations is available through
`stata_test_sureg` (this is `test` after `sureg`, not shown on the UCLA page):

```{julia}
sur73_fit = stata_sureg_fit(fringe, :hrearn => X73, :hrbens => X73)
stata_test_sureg(sur73_fit, ["[hrearn]union", "[hrbens]union"])
```

## Example 7.7 (jtrain1.dta) — pooled OLS and FGLS for the scrap-rate equation

Original Stata code:

```{julia}
#=
use http://www.stata.com/data/jwooldridge/eacsap/jtrain1, clear
reg lscrap d89 d88 grant grant_1
xtgls lscrap d89 d88 grant grant_1, i(fcode)
=#
```

### Pooled OLS

```{julia}
jt77 = DataFrames.dropmissing(jtrain1,
        [:lscrap, :d89, :d88, :grant, :grant_1, :fcode, :year])
DataFrames.nrow(jt77)
```

```{julia}
ols77 = stata_regress(jt77; y = :lscrap, x = [:d89, :d88, :grant, :grant_1])
```

**Comparison.** Exact match with Stata: N = 162, F(4, 157) = 0.69,
Prob > F = 0.5989, R² = 0.0173, Root MSE = 1.4922, and every coefficient /
standard error (`d89` −0.4965236, 0.3379281; `d88` −0.2393704, 0.3108639;
`grant` 0.2000197, 0.3382846; `grant_1` 0.0489357, 0.4360663;
`_cons` 0.5974341, 0.203063).

### FGLS (`xtgls`, homoskedastic panels, no autocorrelation)

```{julia}
gls77 = stata_xtgls(jt77, :lscrap, [:d89, :d88, :grant, :grant_1];
                    panelvar = :fcode, timevar = :year,
                    panels = :iid, corr = :independent)
```

**Comparison.** Exact match with Stata. With `panels(iid) corr(independent)`
FGLS reduces to OLS, so the point estimates equal the OLS ones above, and
`stata_xtgls` reproduces Stata's `xtgls` standard errors to every printed digit:
`d89` 0.3326723, `d88` 0.306029, `grant` 0.3330233, `grant_1` 0.4292842,
`_cons` 0.1999048 — identical to the published table.

## Examples 7.8 and 7.9 (gpa.dta) — term GPA, FGLS and a serial-correlation test

Original Stata code:

```{julia}
#=
use http://www.stata.com/data/jwooldridge/eacsap/gpa, clear
reg trmgpa spring cumgpa crsgpa frstsem season sat verbmath hsperc hssize black female
xtgls trmgpa spring cumgpa crsgpa frstsem season sat verbmath hsperc hssize black female, i(id)
predict res, res
sort id term
by id: gen res1 = res[_n-1]
reg trmgpa cumgpa crsgpa season sat verbmath hsperc hssize black female res1
=#
```

### Pooled OLS (Example 7.8)

```{julia}
X78 = [:spring, :cumgpa, :crsgpa, :frstsem, :season, :sat,
       :verbmath, :hsperc, :hssize, :black, :female]

gpa78 = DataFrames.dropmissing(gpa, vcat([:trmgpa, :id, :term], X78))
DataFrames.sort!(gpa78, [:id, :term])
DataFrames.nrow(gpa78)
```

```{julia}
ols78 = stata_regress(gpa78; y = :trmgpa, x = X78)
```

**Comparison.** Exact match: N = 732, F(11, 720) = 70.64, R² = 0.5191,
Root MSE = 0.52986; `cumgpa` 0.3146158 (0.0404916), `crsgpa` 0.9840371
(0.0960343), `sat` 0.0014097 (0.0001464), `female` 0.2855528 (0.0509641),
`_cons` −2.067599 (0.3381007).

### FGLS (`xtgls`)

```{julia}
gls78 = stata_xtgls(gpa78, :trmgpa, X78;
                    panelvar = :id, timevar = :term,
                    panels = :iid, corr = :independent)
```

**Comparison.** Same picture as Example 7.7: coefficients identical to Stata,
and `stata_xtgls` matches Stata's `xtgls` standard errors to every printed
digit — `spring` 0.0460987, `cumgpa` 0.0401583, `crsgpa` 0.0952439,
`sat` 0.0001452, `black` 0.0538875, `_cons` 0.3353179 — identical to the
published table.

### Example 7.9 — test for serial correlation via the lagged OLS residual

`predict res, res` then `by id: gen res1 = res[_n-1]` builds the within-student
lag; in Julia this is a group-wise shift.

```{julia}
gpa78.res = StatsBase.residuals(ols78, gpa78)

gpa78.res1 = Vector{Union{Missing,Float64}}(missing, DataFrames.nrow(gpa78))
for g in DataFrames.groupby(gpa78, :id)
    for i in 2:DataFrames.nrow(g)
        g.res1[i] = g.res[i - 1]
    end
end

gpa79 = DataFrames.dropmissing(gpa78, :res1)
DataFrames.nrow(gpa79)
```

```{julia}
X79  = [:cumgpa, :crsgpa, :season, :sat, :verbmath,
        :hsperc, :hssize, :black, :female, :res1]
ols79 = stata_regress(gpa79; y = :trmgpa, x = X79)
```

**Comparison.** Exact match with Stata: N = 366, F(10, 355) = 56.83,
R² = 0.6155, Root MSE = 0.48541, and in particular the coefficient of interest
`res1` = 0.1941929 with SE 0.0612068, t = 3.17, p = 0.002 — identical to the
published table. The other slopes also agree to every printed digit
(`cumgpa` 0.3488556/0.0720263, `crsgpa` 1.00062/0.117679,
`_cons` −2.266297/0.4246611).
